coredata.dc:title: Exploring of multicolor emissive carbon dots with novel double emission mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Dong
Liu, Xiaolu
Jiang, Dongli
Zhao, Huan
Zhu, Yuda
Chen, Xiaoqin
Luo, Hongrong
Fan, Hongsong
Zhang, Xingdong
coredata.dc:description: Multicolor emissive carbon dots (CDs) was successfully synthesized via a facile one-pot solvothermal method with the assistance of ethanol, which is nominated as E-CDs. In the reaction system, the addition of ethanol plays a key role in synthesizing CDs with higher proportion of amorphous structures, resulting in multicolor emission of the E-CDs after dissolved in various solvents. Moreover, this solvent dependent multicolor emission of the E-CDs is found to be ascribed to a novel double emission mechanism. The excitation dependent blue emissions stem from intrinsic surface/defect states of the E-CDs while the excitation independent long wavelength emissions originate from solvent molecules connected on the surface of the E-CDs via hydrogen bond. Noticeably, the hydrogen bond between the E-CDs and surrounding environment endows the prepared E-CDs with a unique pH-sensitive feature, which can be used to construct fluorescent pH sensor. Besides, based on this multicolor emission, the E-CDs show potential in bio-labeling, multicolor patterning and solid luminous materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ethanol-assisted synthesis
multicolor emission
Novel double emission mechanism
Fluorescent pH sensor
Cell labeling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the E-CDs and N-CDs
Fluorescence measurements of the E-CDs in different pH
Synthesis and characterization of the E-CDs and N-CDs
Multicolor emissive mechanism of the E-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of the preparation of the E-CDs and N-CDs.
TEM images of (a) E-CDs and (b) N-CDs. (c) Raman spectra of E-CDs (red line) and N-CDs (black line). (d) The prepared E-CDs and N-CDs in various solvents under UV light (365 nm).
Characterizations of the E-CDs: (a) FTIR spectrum; High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d) of the E-CDs.

The fluorescent spectra of the E-CDs in (a) water, (b) DMSO, (c) DMF, (d) methanol under various excitation wavelengths. Insets in (a–d): the optical images of the E-CDs in above solvents under UV lamp (365 nm). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the E-CDs in (e) water and methanol, (f) DMF and DMSO.
Normalized FL excitation (FLE) spectra of the E-CDs in (a) water, (b) DMSO, (c) DMF, (d) methanol.
The fluorescent spectrum of the E-CDs in (a) water and (b) methanol with various pH under excitation wavelength of 360 nm.
(a) Molecular structures of DMF and DMSO and Schematic of esterification reaction between acid and methanol. (b) Schematic of possible double emission mechanisms.

(a) Fluorescence images of MG-63 cells excited by UV, blue, and green light. (b) The photographs of the E-CDs acted as pH sensor under a hand-hold UV light (365 nm). (c) The pictures of different CDs/polymer films under UV lamp (365 nm).

head.ce:title: Exploring of multicolor emissive carbon dots with novel double emission mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicolor emissive carbon dots with novel double emission mechanism and the corresponding applications.

Multicolor emissive carbon dots (CDs) was successfully synthesized via a facile one-pot solvothermal method with the assistance of ethanol, which is nominated as E-CDs. In the reaction system, the addition of ethanol plays a key role in synthesizing CDs with higher proportion of amorphous structures, resulting in multicolor emission of the E-CDs after dissolved in various solvents. Moreover, this solvent dependent multicolor emission of the E-CDs is found to be ascribed to a novel double emission mechanism. The excitation dependent blue emissions stem from intrinsic surface/defect states of the E-CDs while the excitation independent long wavelength emissions originate from solvent molecules connected on the surface of the E-CDs via hydrogen bond. Noticeably, the hydrogen bond between the E-CDs and surrounding environment endows the prepared E-CDs with a unique pH-sensitive feature, which can be used to construct fluorescent pH sensor. Besides, based on this multicolor emission, the E-CDs show potential in bio-labeling, multicolor patterning and solid luminous materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, have attracted tremendous attention in recent years. Owning to their fascinating advantages, such as superior optical properties [1–4], low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility [5–8], CDs have been proved to be one of the most potential candidates in multiple applications, including biomedical imaging [9–13], fluorescent pH sensors [14], lighting devices [15,16] and photocatalysis [17,18]. And in most of these applications, photoluminescence (PL) colors from blue to red are all required.

However, although CDs that can emit PL colors ranging from blue to red have been successfully prepared using diverse routes and raw materials, most of them merely emit one certain color [1,16,19,20], which cannot be changed by external conditions. This stability means that the prepared CDs only possess single fluorescent property, which is limited towards practical applications in some cases. To the best of our knowledge, traditional multicolor emission of the prepared CDs can be achieved via changing excitation wavelength [4,21], but such multicolor emission suffers from paying the price of fluorescent intensity [7,20,22], therefore, it cannot be truly labeled multicolor emission.

Recently, a few publications reported a kinds of multicolor emissive CDs which can emit various PL colors nearly across the entire visible spectra after dissolved in different solvents [23,24]. These solvent-dependent multicolor emissive CDs are remarkable by virtue of it can endow the synthesized CDs with versatility due to their superior optical property. Nevertheless, these solvent-dependent multicolor emissive CDs suffer from lacking traditional blue light emission and poor water solubility, presenting limited potential in biomedical applications [23,24]. What's more, the shortage of reports on CDs samples with truly multicolor emission postpones the process of understanding the mechanisms of truly multicolor emission, thus hampering the rational design of multicolor emissive CDs.

CDs are usually composed of carbon core and amorphous structures [25]. It has been reported that the PL emissions can shift to red with the increase of the proportion of amorphous structures [26,36], which give us inspiration to prepare multicolor emissive CDs via enhancing the proportion of amorphous structures of the prepared CDs. The enhancement of amorphous structures of the CDs means lower degree of dehydration and carbonization towards the precursors. Usually, in a typical solvothermal process, the reaction temperature and time directly contribute to the degree of dehydration and carbonization of reaction precursors. However, it is difficult to reduce the degree of dehydration and carbonization of the reaction precursors by varying the reaction temperature and time, because low reaction temperature and time always indicate blue light emissive molecular-/polymer-like structure instead of nanoparticles [27].

In this work, we choose widely used citric acid and formamide as carbon source and nitrogen source respectively to synthesize truly multicolor emissive CDs by one-pot solvothermal method. Herein, ethanol, a kind of common low-boiling solvent, was added in the reaction system, which acts as reaction accelerator to assist the formation of CDs at a relative low temperature, thus resulting in the achievement of high proportion of amorphous structures of the as-prepared CDs. And by this means, we successfully prepared truly multicolor emissive CDs, which can emit not only strong blue light in water, but also long wavelength lights (yellow- and red-colors) in organic solvents. Moreover, this multicolor emission of the prepared CDs is attributed to a novel double emission mechanism. More interestingly, the E-CDs exhibit a unique pH response, which show potential to fabricate pH sensor. Besides, the excellent PL property makes the E-CDs versatile in applications such as cell-labeling, multicolor patterning and solid luminous materials.

Citric acid and all the organic solvents, including formamide, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC), ethanol, methanol, 1-propanol and 1-butanol, methanoic acid, ammonium hydroxide, poly (vinyl pyrroli-done) (PVP), poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG), and poly (vinylalcohol) (PVA) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. Methyl thiazolyltetrazolium (MTT) was purchased from Amreso, USA. Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) was from Hyclone (USA). All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification. Water was deionized and purified by a water purification system with a resistance of 18.25 MΩ cm (Youpu ULUP-II-10 T, Beijing).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the E-CDs and N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of the E-CDs, 2.0 g of citric acid was dissolved in a mixed solution of ethanol (25 mL) and formamide (25 mL). The formative transparent solution was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon bottle held in a stainless-steel autoclave, and put in an oven for maintaining at 150℃ for 5 h. After the autoclave cooled to room temperature naturally, the resulting black-red solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to collect precipitate. The obtained precipitate was then washed with acetone for several times to remove reaction residue and small molecule particles and further filtered through 0.22 μm filter. Finally, the purified precipitate was dried in a vacuum oven for 12 h to acquire final CDs.
The N-CDs was prepared by the same procedure as for the E-CDs, the only difference is without the addition of ethanol. After drying in vacuum oven, black powder can be obtained.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) micrographs were performed using an FEI Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrophotometer. Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out using a UV 3600. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed on a Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer. Fluorescence quantum yields (QY) were measured on the Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer. X-ray powder diﬀ ;raction (XRD) pattern of the as-prepared samples was collected using a Dandong Fangyuan DX-1000 diffractometer. Raman spectrum was obtained on a LabRAM HR.

The cytotoxicity of the E-CDs was measured through MTT assay. Human osteosarcoma (MG-63) cells (1 *105 cells/well) were seeded in a 24-well plate, and incubated at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 overnight. The E-CDs solution with different concentrations (10, 20, 50, 100 μg/mL, respectively), were introduced into the wells and cultured for another 24 h, 72 h, respectively. Meanwhile, cells without the addition of E-CDs was cultured as control. After incubated for 1 and 3 days, cells were further cultured with 0.5 mg/mL MTT for 4 h at 37℃. Then, the added solution was removed and at the same time, 600 μL of DMSO was joined to dissolve purple formazan salts, in a micro-shaker for 30 min. Finally, the absorbance of the obtained solution was measured by utilizing a multidetection microplate reader (Bio-Teklnstru-ments Inc., USA) at 490 nm. Note that all assays were performed in triplicate.

MG-63 cells (1 *105 cells/well) were seeded in a 24-well plate, and incubated at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 for 24 h. The E-CDs solution with concentration of 100 μg/mL was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and then added to the plates. After incubating the mixture for 12 h, the DMEM medium was removed and the cells were further washed with PBS three times to remove non-speciﬁcally bound E-CDs. The ﬂuorescence images were carried out using ﬂuorescence microscope.

The selected polymers, including poly (vinyl pyrroli-done) (PVP), poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG), and poly (vinylalcohol) (PVA), were mixed with 1 mg/mL of the E-CDs solution (in water). The concentration of the polymers are all 100 mg/mL. After ultrasonic treatment for several hours, the resulting mixture was transferred into a clean glass vial and dried at room temperature to obtain the CDs/polymer composite films.

To obtain solvent modified CDs in power form, as-preapred CDs were dissolved in DMSO, DMF and methanol, respectively. Then, the obtained solutions were treated by reduced pressure evaporation (DMSO-CDs aolution) and rotary evaporating (DMF- and methanol-CDs solutions) to remove solvents. Finally, teh resulting wet products were further lyophilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence measurements of the E-CDs in different pH
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 1 mg of the E-CDs were dissolved in 10 mL of water, DMSO, DMF and methanol, respectively. Then, the different pH values of the obtained solutions were adjusted by utilizing methanoic acid and ammonium hydroxide. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the prepared solutions were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer following various excitation wavelengths.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the E-CDs and N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the effect of added ethanol, we prepared two kinds of CDs using same carbon source and nitrogen source at same reaction conditions, the only difference was the addition of ethanol. For convenience, the CDs adding ethanol is nominated as E-CDs while the one without ethanol is called N-CDs. Fig. 1 illustrates a possible effect of the ethanol in the preparation process of CDs. Ethanol can evaporate at a relative low temperature during the solvothermal reaction due to its low-boiling points. The evaporation of ethanol can boost the pressure in the stainless-steel autoclave, which can assist the formation of the CDs at a relative low temperature, thus decreasing the degree of graphitization and meanwhile increasing the proportion of amorphous structures [28,29].

The morphology of the two CDs were characterized via transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, both the E-CDs and N-CDs are quasi-spherical shape and the averaged diameters of the E-CDs and N-CDs are determined to be 4.5 nm and 5.5 nm, respectively. Although the high-resolution TEM image shows that both the E-CDs and N-CDs possess crystalline lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm (Insets in Fig. 2a and b), which is attributed to the (100) facet of graphite [5,30], the E-CDs shows a less clear fringes compared with that of the N-CDs, implying lower degree of graphitization towards the E-CDs. Moreover, it can be clearly seen that both the XRD spectra of the E-CDs and N-CDs indicate the existence of graphitic structure, however, the intense and broad band at the baseline of the E-CDs implies a large amount of amorphous structures while the narrow peak of the N-CDs suggests higher degree of graphitization (Fig. S1). Raman spectra in Fig. 2c further confirm above results. The intensity ratio of ID/IG is found to increase from 0.88 to 1.13 for N-CDs to E-CDs, indicating that the addition of ethanol successfully enhances the proportion of amorphous structures. Subsequently, the fluorescent properties of the two prepared CDs were preliminarily measured. As shown in Fig. 2d, the E-CDs displays multicolor emissions after dissolved in different solvents while the N-CDs shows a common stable blue light emission. These findings suggests that through a rational design towards a common solvothermal method, CDs with high proportion of amorphous structures was successfully prepared in this work. Moreover, the amorphous structures indeed have the potential to induce long wavelength emissions, which is in consistent with our previous hypothesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor emissive mechanism of the E-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to the estimation of PL properties by UV light, multicolor emissive E-CDs merited further investigation, therefore, the chemical structures and PL property of the E-CDs are thoroughly explored. Firstly, FT-IR spectrum was measured to investigate the surface states and chemical compositions of E-CDs. Peaks at 1641, 1597, and 1347 cm−1 are ascribed to stretching vibrations of CN∕CO, CC, and CN, respectively, suggesting the formation of poly- aromatic structures in the CDs (Fig. 3 a). The peaks at 1196 and 1096 cm−1 are due to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC. The peaks at 2987 and 2802 cm−1 stem from CH stretching vibrations of sp2 and sp3. And two broad peaks located at 3383 and 3142 cm−1 indicate the presence of OH and NH. And XPS spectra further confirm the results of FT-IR. The full scan XPS spectrum shows three typical peaks: C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV) and O1s (531 eV) (Fig. S2), indicating that the CDs are composed of C, N and O elements. In the high resolution XPS spectrum, the C1s band can be deconvoluted into four distinct peaks: CC (284.5 eV), CN (286.4 eV), CO (287.1 eV) and C = N∕C = O (288.7 eV) (Fig. 3b). The N1s band can be deconvoluted into three binding energies at 399.1 eV, 399.6 eV and 401.3 eV, representing pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. 3c). Two fitting peaks of O1s band located at 530.7 eV and 532.4 eV are attributed to CO and CO bonds respectively (Fig. 3d). By combining all these aforementioned characterization data and analyses, the E-CDs can be considered to be composed of a graphite-like carbon cores and a large amount of amorphous structures on the surface with abundant functional groups.

On the other hand, the surface states and chemical compositions of the N-CDs were also studied. As shown in Fig. S3a, N-CDs suffer from less functional groups on their surface such as NH and CO, compared to that of E-CDs. This conclusion was also supported by high resolution XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. S3c–d, N-CDs possess relatively low content of amino N (Fig. S3c) and CO (Fig. S3d). Therefore, we can tentatively draw the conclusion that E-CDs have richer functional groups on the surface compared with N-CDs. And this structure difference lay the foundation for surface bonding between E-CDs and solvents.

Subsequently, optical property of the E-CDs was thoroughly investigated. As mentioned above, the E-CDs displayed multicolour emissions after dissolved in four kinds of solvents: water, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and methanol. The corresponding emission spectra are presented in Fig. 4 a–d. E-CDs shows blue-dominated and excitation dependent emission in water (Fig. 4a), while displays a double-band emissions in DMSO, DMF and methanol (Fig. 4b–d). It is worth noting that the long wavelength emissions in the three solvents are excitation independent and sensitive to solvents while the emissions in blue-green region are excitation dependent and insensitive to solvents, which are similar to water. This finding indicates that the blue-green region emissions in the four solvents stem from same origin, and apparently, the long wavelength emissions in organic solvents originate from another origin.

The different natures of the blue band emission and long wavelength emissions are confirmed by the UV–vis spectra and fluorescent excitation (FLE) spectra. The absorption band located at about 360 nm appear in all the UV spectra (Fig. 4e–f), which is frequently attributed to the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states [31,32], usually generating blue emission. However, the UV spectra of the E-CDs in methanol, DMSO and DMF show strong absorption band at visible region (≈400-750 nm) (Fig. 4e red line and f), indicating that the long wavelength emissions of the E-CDs in DMSO, DMF and methanol should be assigned to the absorption bands at visible region, which is usually ascribed to the transition of surface groups [24,26]. The above results are demonstrated by FLE spectra. Similar excitation peaks are found when following the emission peaks between 440–450 nm in the four solvents (Fig. 5 ), suggesting same emission nature. However, the excitation peaks following long wavelength emissions (550 nm in DMSO, 547 nm in DMF, 616 nm in methanol) are found not only extremely unlike from the one following blue-light emission, indicating them coming from different emission states, but also well overlapped with the UV absorbance bands. For example, the excitation peak at 535 nm following emission peak at 616 nm in methanol matches well with its absorption band located at 533 nm (Fig. 4e; Fig. 5d). These results indicate that the absorption bands at visible region should be responsible for the long wavelength emissions. In addition, averaged lifetimes of long wavelength emission peaks (550 nm in DMSO, 547 nm in DMF and 616 nm in methanol) are found to decrease with the red shift of the excitation wavelength (Fig. S4), which is opposite to emissions induced by surface states [24,33]. Moreover, although a triple exponentials fitting has been found in DMF, DMSO and methanol under the long wavelength excitation (505 nm), one of the concrete components contribute a tremendous part with proportions of 90.48％ in DMSO, 84.57％ in DMF and 91.66％ in methanol, respectively (Table S1), which indicate that only one radiative transition channel is mostly responsible for the PL emission when excited by long wavelengths [30]. Consequently, we can conclude that the excitation dependent blue band emission and the excitation independent long wavelength emission in DMSO, DMF and methanol stem from different emission states, which are tentatively assigned to surface sates and surface groups, respectively.

However, although the E-CDs in water can generate blue-dominated excitation dependent emission, it fails to produce long wavelength emission, it can be inferred that E-CDs itself does not contain fluorescent groups which can generate long wavelength emission. Therefore, the long wavelength emissions in DMSO, DMF and methanol may stem from extraneous fluorescent group connected on the surface of the E-CDs, which probably come from solvents.

Based on above analyses, pH experiments were carried out, aiming at further confirming the natures of surface states emission and extraneous fluorescent groups induced emission. Firstly, the nature of surface states emission was proved. After dissolved in water, PL intensity of peak emission in blue band shows great response to pH value (Fig. 6 a). The PL intensity in acid region is stronger than that in basic region. And in acid region, the PL gradually enhances with pH increasing from 4 to 6, while in basic region, the PL decays gradually as pH value increasing from 8 to 10. And the same pH response happens to emission peak at blue band in methanol (Fig. 6b). The PL intensity of blue-green band emission display high pH sensitivity, which is frequently attributed to alterations of surface charge caused by protonation and deprotonation [34,35], indicating their same nature of surface states emission.

Subsequently, the pH effect towards long wavelength emissions were carried out. In DMSO and DMF, the addition of acid or base has little influence on the emission peaks at around 550 nm (Fig. S5 and S6), indicating its nature of stable extraneous fluorescent group induced emission. However, in methanol, after the addition of base, a blue shift presents to the long wavelength emission peak at 616 nm (Fig. 6b; more detail in Fig. S7b), moving from 616 nm to 590 nm, while after joining acid, this emission peak disappears directly (Fig. S7a). At the same time, after joining base in DMSO and DMF, excitation independent emission peaks located at around 625 nm appear (Fig. S5b and S6b). Furthermore, the addition of base in water similarly results in the appearance of new excitation independent emission peak at around 585 nm (Fig. 6a; more detail in Fig. S8b). These results indicate that the joined base, probably hydroxyl, should be responsible for emergence of the two emission peaks. Therefore, we tentatively draw the conclusion that the long wavelength emission at 616 nm in methanol originates from hydroxyl groups. Experiments were carried out to confirm our hypothesis. E-CDs were then dissolved in ethanol, 1-propanol and 1-butanol. E-CDs displays almost similar fluorescent spectra in all these alcoholic solvents (Fig. S9a-c), and the UV–vis spectrum also shows similar graphs for all the alcohol solvents (Fig. S9d). The results demonstrate above proposal, and also give reasonable explanation for the blue shift of the emission peak at 616 nm after the addition of base, which should be ascribed to the difference between the intrinsic hydroxyl in methanol and added hydroxyl from base. And the disappearance of emission peak at 616 nm after the addition of acid is due to consumption of hydroxyl caused by an esterification reaction between alcohol and acid.

There is no doubt that the long wavelength emission in methanol stems from hydroxyl, which have been reported recently [23]. Then, we further explored the emissions at around 550 nm in DMSO and DMF. It has been reported that the different polarity of DMF and DMSO can induce different fluorescent emission, according to the solvatochromism effect [23], but the emission peaks in DMSO and DMF are of high similarity (Fig. 4b-c), suggesting that the polarity does not work. Moreover, whether the solvent is protic or aprotic can contribute to the fluorescence [24]. DMSO and DMF are both aprotic solvents, therefore, fluorescent spectrum of the CDs in protic solvent formamide (FA) was measured, which is found to be consistent with fluorescent spectra of DMF and DMSO (Fig. S10a; Fig. 3b–c), thus denying this assumption.

We hence spontaneously suspect that the long wavelength emissions at 550 nm in DMSO and 547 nm in DMF stem from solvents molecules. DMSO and DMF share similar molecular structures of oxygen-containing double bonds (CO/SO) (Fig. 7 a). To our knowledge, the introduction of oxygen-containing double bonds can cause electron transition, and subsequently generates new energy level, resulting in long wavelength emission [26,27,36]. The CDs was then dissolved in acetone, which contains CO bond. The fluorescent spectrum is highly consistent with that in DMSO and DMF (Fig. S10b), which preliminarily verifies above hypothesis. Meanwhile, an excitation independent emission peak at around 535 nm appears after adding acid in methanol (Fig. 6b; more detail in Fig. S7a), which should be assigned to the structure containing CO bond generated by esterification reaction (Fig. 7a). Furthermore, hydrochloric acid (HCl) without CO groups was chosen to further confirm this assumption. No new emission peak emerges after joining HCl (Fig. S11). All in all, the emission peak at around 550 nm in DMSO and DMF should be ascribed to oxygen-containing double bonds in their molecules. The introduction of oxygen-containing double bonds can generate new energy level, leading to long wavelength emissions.

The aforementioned surface modifications of solvent-treated CDs are confirmed via FTIR and XPS data. As shown in Fig. S12a, after treated by methanol, DMF and DMSO, there are some changes happened to FTIR spectra of E-CDs, for example, the characteristic absorption band of CC occurs at 1597 cm−1 for bare CDs and at a smaller wavenumber of 1557 cm−1 for the solvents-treated CDs. In addition, the DMSO-treated CDs give rise to an absorption band located at 1020 cm−1, which are attribute to SO bond. Moreover, according to XPS spectrum, the S peak also occurs for the DMSO-treated CDs (Fig. S12b). These results indicate that DMSO molecules successfully connected with E-CDs. In the high resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S13), although no new groups or structures are observed after treated by solvents because the solvents and E-CDs share similar elements and groups, there still exist some alterations. For example, the DMF-treated CDs have a relatively high content of CN bond (Fig. S13a) while the methanol-treated CDs possess more CO bond (Fig. S13c), which should be ascribed to DMF and methanol molecules connected on the E-CDs. The FTIR and XPS data confirmed that the solvent molecules can interact with E-CDs, yielding modulated optical properties of the E-CDs.

Based on the results above, herein, a schematic graph is exhibited to help understand the double emission mechanisms. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the strong blue and excitation dependent emission of the E-CDs stem from intrinsic surface states, while the excitation independent long wavelength emissions in DMSO, DMF and methanol should be attributed to their molecules connected on the surface of the E-CDs. Solvent molecules can connect on the surface of the E-CDs via hydrogen bond between CDs according to the analyses of FTIR and XPS, which provide possibility for hydrogen bonding between E-CDs and solvent molecules. In methanol, the existence of hydroxyl can form stable excited states, while in DMSO and DMF, oxygen-containing double bonds in their molecules can generate new stable energy level, these stable emissive states result in excitation independent long wavelength emissions. Herein, it should be note that the formation of hydrogen bond has a close relationship with the large amount of amorphous structures of the E-CDs. To the best of our knowledge, the large amount of amorphous structures may imply vast and abundant function groups on the surface of the CDs, which lay a foundation for the formation of hydrogen bond between the E-CDs and solvents molecules.

The prepared E-CDs displays versatility in various applications. In order to make full use of this multicolor emissions of the prepared E-CDs, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the E-CDs were measured and found to be 15.81% in water (blue light), 22.43% in DMSO (yellow light), 25.80% in DMF (yellow light) and 19.42% in methanol (red light) respectively, which are comparable to previous CDs [7,30].

Firstly, the prepared CDs were used as cell labeling agents. MTT assay displays low cytotoxicity of the E-CDs (Fig. S14). After co-culture, MG-63 cells were successfully labelled by E-CDs, and displayed bright blue, green and red colors under different excitation (Fig. 8 a), demonstrating that the E-CDs possess great potential to substitute organic dyes or semiconductor QDs in bio-labeling. Moreover, based on this excitation-dependent emission in water, we fabricated photo-crosslinking PEGDA/CDs hydrogel composites, and multicolor patterning can be observed under various excitations (Fig. S15).

Then, based on the unique pH response, the E-CDs can be used to intuitively detect the acid-base property of environmental water. As shown in Fig.8b, the color of water after adding the E-CDs can directly display whether the environmental water is acid or basic without other measurements or characterizations.

The E-CDs is featured by solvent-dependent multicolor emission, which shows potential in solid luminous materials. Herein, three polymers including poly (vinyl pyrroli-done) (PVP; CO), poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG; OH), and poly (vinylalcohol) (PVA; OH) were chosen to make CDs/polymer films, based on their function groups which are similar to above-mentioned solvents. The emissive colors of CDs/polymer films are found to be highly in accord with corresponding solvents (Fig. 8c).

In this work, we have synthesized truly multicolor emissive CDs through a rational design towards a solvothermal method. Low-boiling ethanol was added in the reaction system, aiming at facilitating the formation of CDs at a relative low temperature, under which CDs with high proportion of amorphous structures and low degree of graphitization can be obtained, and the prepared CDs displayed multicolor emissions after dissolved in various solvents. Moreover, the multicolor emission of the E-CDs is found to be attributed to a novel double emission mechanism, which have not been reported so far. The excitation dependent blue emissions stem from intrinsic surface states of the E-CDs, while the excitation independent long wavelength emissions originate from solvent molecules connected on the surface of the E-CDs via hydrogen bond. This hydrogen bond endows the prepared E-CDs with a unique pH-sensitive feature, which can be used to construct fluorescent pH sensor. Besides, based on this multicolor emission, applications such as bio-labeling, multicolor patterning and solid luminous film can also be achieved.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51673128 and 51503127).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.09.031.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging

Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hongsong Fan obtained her Ph.D. in National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials in Sichuan University, and now is professor of National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, Sichuan University. Her academic research focuses on novel biomaterials for tissue regeneration and multifunctional nano materials for biological application.
